─[ Platform Integration Reliability vs Cost Audit ]─ ┐

Platform Integration Reliability vs Cost Audit

Why this brainstorm exists

We needed a blunt review of Lore integration quality across Claude Code, OpenCode, and Cursor, with focus on context payload size, injection frequency, and whether prompt engineering at each hook surface is cost-effective.

High-level verdict

  • Claude Code currently has the best reliability vs cost balance.
  • OpenCode has the largest cost issue due to full banner injection on every system transform call.
  • Cursor is workable but constrained by platform limits and has implementation contradictions that reduce reliability.

Evidence reviewed

  • Hook and plugin wiring files for all three platforms.
  • Banner and rules generation code and static rule payloads.
  • Hook logging pipeline and analyzer.
  • Live hook event log data from this repo (.git/lore-hook-events.jsonl).

Measured payload findings

  • buildBanner() payload is about 14,067 characters.
  • OpenCode currently injects that full payload in experimental.chat.system.transform every call.
  • Sample log analysis: 34 chat.system.transform fires produced about 491,580 injected characters from that hook alone.
  • Cursor always-on rules total about 20,075 characters (lore-core, lore-project, lore-delegation, work-tracking).
  • Cursor dynamic banner path exists (buildCursorBanner()), but current session hook uses full buildBanner().

Platform-specific assessment

Claude Code

  • Strong injection surfaces: session start, per-prompt, pre-tool, post-tool.
  • Pattern is mostly sane: one heavy startup payload plus smaller recurring nudges.
  • Best current tradeoff for reliability vs token cost.

OpenCode

  • Reliability is high because system transform runs every call.
  • Cost profile is currently poor because full banner is re-injected per call.
  • “No accumulation” in history does not mean no token cost at inference time.

Cursor

  • Platform limitations are real: weak context injection outside sessionStart.
  • Lore mitigation is clever (rules + hooks + MCP), but several pieces are misaligned.
  • There is contradiction between known platform limits and current assumptions in hook comments/flow.

Notable implementation issues

  • OpenCode: full buildBanner() in per-call system transform is the biggest cost mistake.
  • Cursor: session-init uses full banner instead of minimal cursor banner.
  • Cursor: MCP server references getAgentDomains from lib/banner.js, but that export is missing.
  • Cursor: duplicate/overlapping rule content increases baseline instruction load.
  • Docs and runbooks contain behavior statements that do not match current runtime behavior in key places.

Easy wins

  • OpenCode: keep full banner for startup/compaction, switch per-call transform to a compact reminder.
  • Cursor: use buildCursorBanner() for sessionStart dynamic payload.
  • Cursor: remove or merge redundant always-on rule files.
  • Cursor MCP: fix missing export mismatch so lore_context remains reliable.
  • Documentation: align platform guide and testing runbook with actual code behavior.

Current ranking

  1. Claude Code (best reliability vs cost)
  2. Cursor (medium reliability, moderate to high baseline cost)
  3. OpenCode (high reliability, highest ongoing cost)

Open questions

  • What should be the target character/token budget per injection point by platform?
  • Which context sections should be static, dynamic, or on-demand only?
  • Should profile-based tuning (minimal/balanced/strict) be added after baseline reductions are complete?
PgUp/PgDn: scroll b: bulk commands q: quit x: menu ← → ↑ ↓: navigate
